coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal sensor for arginine detection by inhibiting the growth of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots composite
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ting
Li, Na
Dong, Jiang Xue
Zhang, Ying
Fan, Yu Zhu
Lin, Shu Min
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description:
A bidimensional optical sensing platform which combines the advantages of fluorescence and colorimetry has been designed for arginine (Arg) detection. The system was established by monitoring the influence of Arg on the growth of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots (Au/CQDs) composite, and the CQDs synthesized by ethylene glycol were used as the reducing and stabilizing agent in this paper. Considering that Arg is the only amino acid with guanidine group and has the highest isoelectric point (pI) value at 10.76, Arg would carry positive charges at pH 7.4. Consequently, the positively charged guanidine group of Arg could attract
A u C l
4
−
and CQDs through electrostatic interaction, which inhibited the growth of Au/CQDs composite. Thereby, the color of the system almost did not change and the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was prevented in the presence of Arg. Based on the color change a low detection limit for Arg was 37nM, and a detection limit of 450nM was obtained by fluorescence spectroscopy. Moreover, this dual-signal sensor also revealed excellent selectivity toward Arg over other amino acids. Besides, Arg can be detected in urine samples with satisfactory results, which demonstrate the potential applications for real analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Arginine
Isoelectric point

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots composite
Characterizations of CQDs and Au/CQDs composite
Sensitivity and selectivity for arginine detection
Determination of arginine in human urine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174 ) and the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2013jjB00002 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A) and HRTEM (B) images of the prepared CQDs. Inset of (A) presents the size distributions of CQDs. TEM (C) and HRTEM (D) images of the synthesized Au/CQDs composite. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is shown as the overlay at the bottom portion of the panel (C). Absorption (E) and fluorescence (F) spectra of the CQDs and Au/CQDs composite.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs synthesized at different blending ratios of ethylene glycol and sodium hydroxide (curves a–f). Inset: photographs of the CQDs under UV light (365nm). (B) Absorption spectra of the Au/CQDs composite synthesized with different CQDs that were prepared at different blending ratios of ethylene glycol and sodium hydroxide (curves a–f). Inset: photographs of the Au/CQDs composite solution under natural light. The blending ratios of ethylene glycol and 1M sodium hydroxide from a to f were: 100:0, 100:1, 100:2, 100:5, 100:7 and 10:1.

TEM (A) and HRTEM images (B) of the Au/CQDs composite formed in the presence of Arg. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the system upon various concentrations of Arg (from top to bottom: 0.1, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5μM). (D) Relative absorption intensity dependent on the concentration of Arg within the range of 0–10μM, the linear range for Arg is 0.1–5μM. (E) Fluorescence spectra of Au/CQDs composite in the presence of different concentrations of Arg (from bottom to top: 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5μM). (F) Relative fluorescence intensity over the concentration of Arg from 0 to 10μM, and the linear range for Arg is 1–5μM. All experiments were conducted under the following conditions: 25μL of HAuCl4 (0.1%), 20μL of CQDs, 450μL of phosphate buffer (10mM, pH 7.4), and 5μL of Arg with various concentrations incubated at 37°C for 10min.

Selectivity analysis for Arg detection by the relative absorption and fluorescence intensity of the sensing system. The photograph is the colorimetric response for different amino acids. Volume of HAuCl4 (0.1%): 25μL, volume of CQDs: 20μL, volume of phosphate buffer (10mM, pH 7.4): 450μL, concentration of each amino acid: 5μM, incubation temperature: 37°C, incubation time: 10min.
Schematic illustration for the detection of Arg by CQDs and HAuCl4 with both colorimetric and fluorometric readout.
Comparison of various probes employed for Arg sensing.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal sensor for arginine detection by inhibiting the growth of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A bidimensional optical sensing platform which combines the advantages of fluorescence and colorimetry has been designed for arginine (Arg) detection. The system was established by monitoring the influence of Arg on the growth of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots (Au/CQDs) composite, and the CQDs synthesized by ethylene glycol were used as the reducing and stabilizing agent in this paper. Considering that Arg is the only amino acid with guanidine group and has the highest isoelectric point (pI) value at 10.76, Arg would carry positive charges at pH 7.4. Consequently, the positively charged guanidine group of Arg could attract
A u C l
4
−
and CQDs through electrostatic interaction, which inhibited the growth of Au/CQDs composite. Thereby, the color of the system almost did not change and the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was prevented in the presence of Arg. Based on the color change a low detection limit for Arg was 37nM, and a detection limit of 450nM was obtained by fluorescence spectroscopy. Moreover, this dual-signal sensor also revealed excellent selectivity toward Arg over other amino acids. Besides, Arg can be detected in urine samples with satisfactory results, which demonstrate the potential applications for real analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Arginine (Arg) is the most alkaline amino acid and has the highest isoelectric point (pI) at 10.76 compared with other 20 amino acids, and it plays an important role in cell division, wound healing, protein production, immune responses and many other biological functions (Cao et al., 2014; Stechmiller et al., 2005; Tapiero et al., 2002; Vermeulen et al., 2007). Moreover, Arg is the physiological precursor of nitric oxide (NO), which plays as a key mediator in vascular homeostasis (Gopalakrishnan et al., 1996; Guelzim et al., 2011). When the amount of Arg-derived species is abnormal, it can be unhealthy and even life threatening (He et al., 2014). Thus, the detection of Arg is very significant. Although many strategies for the determination of Arg have been reported, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Gopalakrishnan et al., 1996), gas chromatography (GC) (Williams et al., 1993), and capillary electrophoresis (Olson et al., 1999), most of them need specialized equipments and complicated laboratory procedures. Under these circumstances, to develop a simple, inexpensive, highly sensitive and selective method for the detection of Arg seems particularly necessary.

As a new kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10nm (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn increasing attention in recent years because of their biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity, and excellent fluorescence properties (Lim et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013). With the emergence of CQDs, they provide an exciting opportunity for alternative semiconductor quantum dots that are usually composed of toxic heavy metals (Ding et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2013). CQDs are also applied in many other fields, for example, bioimaging (Cao et al., 2007), fluorescent sensors (Zhang et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013) and photocatalysis (Liu et al., 2014). In addition, CQDs have promising potential to be an oxidizing or reducing agent as they are excellent electron acceptors and electron donors (Strauss et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2009, 2013). The hydroxy and carboxy groups on the surface of CQDs play an important role in the reduction performance of CQDs and lead them to be the nucleation centers for the growth of metallic nanoparticles (Dey et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2013). Herein, CQDs function as the reducing agent like citrate, ascorbates and sodium borohydride.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are the most stable metal nanoparticles (Daniel and Astruc, 2004), and have been widely used for chemical and biological sensing with tremendous applications in nanomedicine (Dykman and Khlebtsov, 2012; Murphy et al., 2008). AuNPs were conventionally synthesized by reducing chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) with reducing agents (Wang et al., 2013). A classical method for AuNPs preparation was introduced, in which HAuCl4 was reduced by citrate in water (Turkevich et al., 1951). However, many previous methods need additional energy (light irradiation or heating) and are time-consuming for the synthesis of AuNPs (Cao et al., 2011; Raveendran et al., 2003; Wang et al., 2013). Consequently, researches on the easy preparation of AuNPs still remain challenged.

In this paper, we successfully synthesized reductive CQDs by single-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs were synthesized using ethylene glycol as carbon sources and show strong reducing power under alkaline environment based on the report (Deng et al., 2014). The obtained CQDs do not need further processing and any additional reducers and stabilizers for the synthesis of AuNPs. The prepared Au/CQDs composite is very stable and can be maintained for 3 months at room temperature. Recently, many AuNPs-based colorimetric methods for Arg detection are realized by directly or indirectly inducing the aggregation of AuNPs and making the solution color changed from red to blue (Patel and Menon, 2009; Pu et al., 2013; Rawat and Kailasa 2014). We explored a new strategy to detect Arg by inhibiting the growth of Au/CQDs composite. With the addition of Arg, the formation of Au/CQDs composite was restrained. As a result, the color of the mixture solution almost did not change and the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was prevented. Based on these features, a highly sensitive and selective dual-signal (colorimetric and fluorometric) sensor for the detection of Arg was constructed.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O, CAS: 16903-35-8), ethylene glycol (CAS: 107-21-1), and sodium hydroxide (CAS: 1310-73-2) were obtained from Aladdin Ltd., Shanghai, China. Amino acids including arginine (Arg, CAS: 74-79-3), asparagines (Asn, CAS: 70-47-3), aspartic acid (Asp, CAS: 56-84-8), cysteine (Cys, CAS: 52-90-4), glutamine (Gln, CAS: 56-85-9), histidine (His, CAS: 71-00-1), leucine (Leu, CAS: 61-90-5), lysine (Lys, CAS: 56-87-1), methionine (Met, CAS: 63-68-3), phenylalanine (Phe, CAS: 63-91-2), proline (Pro, CAS: 147-85-3), and tryptophan (Trp, CAS: 73-22-3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (USA). Aminoguanidine hydrochloride (AG, CAS: 1937-19-5), agmatine sulfate salt (AGM, CAS: 2482-00-0), guanidineacetic acid (GAA, CAS: 352-97-6), and guanidinopropionic acid (GPA, CAS: 353-09-3) were ordered from Shanghai D&B Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan), which equipped with a 150-W xenon lamp, the PMT voltage was 400V. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were measured with a JEOL-2100 system operated at 200kV (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a D8 DISCOVER X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Germany). Raman spectra were measured using a Renishaw 1000 microspectrometer (UK) with an excitation wavelength of 532nm from a 50mW laser.

The synthesis of CQDs prepared through hydrothermal method was briefly described as follows: ethylene glycol and 1M sodium hydroxide were mixed in a ratio of 10:1 and placed in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25mL), and then the mixture was heated at 180°C for 2h. After reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then, the pale yellow products were dialyzed (Mw 500) against water for 24h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots composite
ce:section.ce:para: The Au/CQDs composite was synthesized by the addition of 455μL of phosphate buffer (10mM, pH 7.4) to 25μL of HAuCl4 (0.1%) solution firstly, then 20μL of CQDs (0.025mgmL−1) was added as the reducing and stabilizing agents. The resulting mixture was incubated at 37°C for 10min, when it turned pink, the Au/CQDs composites were formed.

At first, 5μL of Arg within a concentration range of 0–1mM was added to 25μL of HAuCl4 (0.1%) solution. After vigorous shaking, 450μL of phosphate buffer (10mM, pH 7.4) and 20μL of CQDs (0.025mgmL−1) were added successively. The mixture solution was incubated at 37°C for 10min as well, and then the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution were recorded. As shown in Scheme 1, when fluorescent CQDs are added to the HAuCl4 solution, the color of the mixture solution turns to red within 3min and the fluorescence of CQDs is quenched simultaneously. However, when Arg is added to the HAuCl4 solution at first, the Au/CQDs composite can not be formed after the addition of fluorescent CQDs. Herein, the mixture solution is nearly colorless and the fluorescence quenching extent of CQDs was reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of CQDs and Au/CQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and the size distribution of CQDs are presented in Fig. 1A. The CQDs are uniform and well-dispersed with a size distribution around 1–4nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CQDs (Fig. 1B) reveals high crystallinity with the lattice spacing of 0.21nm, which agrees with the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) (Deng et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2013). Moreover, TEM was used to characterize the morphology and size of Au/CQDs composite (Fig. 1C). The synthesized Au/CQDs composites possess larger size than CQDs, and the clear lattice spacing of 0.24nm on HRTEM image (Fig. 1D) displays the (111) lattice space of crystalline Au (Liu et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2015), indicating that the Au/CQDs composite has been reduced by CQDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Au/CQDs composite (Fig. 1C, inset) shows the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of metallic Au, respectively. And the broad diffraction peak at 25° is the characteristic peak of carbon (Liu et al., 2014), which further demonstrates the formation of Au/CQDs composite.

As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs exhibit a typical excitation-dependent feature with increasing excitation wavelength in the range of 325–375nm (Deng et al., 2015). The maximum excitation and emission peaks of CQDs were located at 365 and 450nm, respectively. The absorption spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 1E) has a strong peak at 260nm attributing to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (Dong et al., 2013; Zhang and Chen, 2014). Another broad absorption (300–400nm) is from the trapping of excited state energy by the surface state, resulting in strong fluorescence (Anilkumar et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2010). The typical surface plasmon resonance absorption peak at 525nm (Fig. 1E) of the HAuCl4 and CQDs mixture solution confirms the formation of Au/CQDs composite (Ghosh et al., 2004; Sylvestre et al., 2004). Furthermore, the dramatic fluorescence quenching of CQDs (Fig. 1F) also demonstrates the reduction of Au3+ salt (Mandani et al., 2015). The high fluorescence emission of CQDs is attributed to the radiative recombination of their surface-confined electrons and holes (Ding et al., 2014). When the Au3+ salt was reduced by CQDs, the nucleation was initiated at the surface sites that host the electrons, thereby the electron–hole recombination processes on CQDs surface was disrupted, leading to a fluorescence quenching effect (Mandani et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2012).

As shown in the FTIR spectra of Fig. S2, the functional groups on the surface of CQDs are compared with those of the Au/CQDs composite. It is observed that the stretching vibrational peak of O–H at 3380cm−1 region in the case of CQDs gets narrowed when CQDs were involved in the formation of Au/CQDs composite. The in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrations of O–H at 1300–1500 and 644cm−1 are disappeared, respectively. Furthermore, the intensity of C=O vibrational absorption band at 1670cm−1 is decreased. These results suggest that the hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups on the surface of CQDs have a significant effect on the formation of Au/CQDs composite. The Raman spectrum of CQDs in Fig. S3 does not show any peaks, which may be due to the strong fluorescence background. But the Au/CQDs composites reduced by CQDs show obvious D-band and G-band of carbon at 1351 and 1589cm−1 compared to pure CQDs, attributing to the surface enhancement Raman scattering effect of Au/CQDs composite (Liu et al., 2014; Mandani et al., 2015).

Considering that the alkaline environment may have a great impact on the characters of CQDs in this work, the CQDs synthesized at different ratios of ethylene glycol to sodium hydroxide were compared ( Fig. 2). With increasing concentration of sodium hydroxide, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs rises after the first drop, and then decreases (Fig. 2A), corresponding to the photographs of the CQDs (Fig. 2A, inset) under UV light (365nm). Fig. 2B shows the UV–vis absorption spectra and photographs (under natural light) of the prepared Au/CQDs composite, which were synthesized using the CQDs mentioned above. Obviously, the CQDs obtained using ethylene glycol and 1M sodium hydroxide as precursors at the ratio of 10:1 have the best reduction ability. Thus, the CQDs synthesized by this ratio were chosen as the reducing agent in the following works.

The assay conditions, such as the volumes of CQDs solution and HAuCl4 (0.1%), and reaction time, were optimized for Arg detection. The influence of CQDs and HAuCl4 volumes on the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) and relative absorption intensity (A/A 0) were discussed and displayed in Fig. S4. Distinctly, the F/F 0 and A/A 0 have the optimum values when the volume of CQDs was 20μL (Figs. S4A, S4B). The volume change of HAuCl4 (0.1%) leads to a small difference of A/A 0 (Fig. S4D), but a maximum F/F 0 value at 25μL (Fig. S4C). Herein, 20μL of CQDs and 25μL of HAuCl4 (0.1%) were chosen as the optimum values. The Au/CQDs composite synthesized by CQDs can be stable after 10min, and the absorbance change can be seen within 3min (Fig. S5A). However, when Arg (5μL, 500μM) was added to the HAuCl4 solution before CQDs, the Au/CQDs composite almost can not be synthesized within 45min (Fig. S5B). It is suggested that the reaction time of 10min is suitable for the formation of Au/CQDs composite and has no impact on the sensitivity for Arg detection.

Considering that Arg can inhibit the synthesis of Au/CQDs composite, the mechanism was investigated by TEM image. As shown in Fig. 3A, the obvious aggregation phenomenon is observed when the Au/CQDs composite is formed in the presence of Arg, which may be ascribed to that the pH value of buffer solution (7.4) is much lower than the pI value of Arg (10.76). Because the guanidine group has strongly basic character and will be fully protonated under physiological conditions (Feichtinger et al., 1998), the positively charged guanidine group of Arg will interact with
A u C l
4
−
and CQDs through electrostatic interaction. Hence, Au3+ can not be reduced immediately and needs longer time to form stable Au/CQDs composite (Patel and Menon, 2009; Pu et al., 2013; Rawat and Kailasa 2014). In order to confirm this hypothesis, we observed the influence of Arg on the formation of Au/CQDs composite under higher pH values (Fig. S6). When pH value was adjusted to 9.0, Arg almost can not restrain the formation of Au/CQDs composite. This is because the positive charge of Arg was decreased, leading to the weakening of the electrostatic interaction of Arg. Furthermore, we investigated whether Lys could be detected by adjusting the pH values for the pI value of Lys is also high (9.7). As illustrated in Fig. S7, the presence of Lys can not prevent the formation of Au/CQDs composite by adjusting pH but Arg can. These results prove that the restraint on the formation of Au/CQDs composite by Arg can not be just due to the electrostatic interaction, the special guanidine group of Arg may also play a key role (Pu et al., 2013). Thus, we investigated some other chemicals with guanidine group including AG, AGM, GAA, and GPA (Fig. S8). Interestingly, like Arg, they all prevented the formation of Au/CQDs composite. Based on all results mentioned above, it draws a conclusion that the guanidine group of Arg is the key role to inhibit Au/CQDs composite formation through electrostatic interaction. From the HRTEM image (Fig. 3B), the aggregated CQDs with size below 5nm demonstrate that the Au/CQDs composite has not been formed. Simultaneously, the fluorescence of CQDs remained unquenched. Thus, both colorimetric and fluorometric signals can be used for the detection of Arg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for arginine detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum conditions discussed above, the linear response range of Arg was measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the absorption peak centered at 520nm decreases gradually with increasing concentration of Arg. There has a good linear correlation (R2=0.980) between relative absorbance and the concentration of Arg in the range of 0.1–5μM (Fig. 3D), the detection limit of Arg based on 3δ/slope was approximately 37nM, which is lower than those of most methods reported for detection of Arg in Table 1. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the mixed solution had some extent of increase with the concentration of Arg (Fig. 3E). The Arg inhibits the formation of Au/CQDs composite, thereby the fluorescence of CQDs is not quenched totally (Mandani et al., 2015). Fig. 3F presents a favorable linear relationship (R2=0.993) between relative fluorescence intensity and the Arg concentration in the range of 1–5μM, the limit of the detection (defined as 3δ/slope) was estimated to be 450nM. By comparison, Arg (5μL, 500μM) was also added to the prepared Au/CQDs composite solution (Fig. S9). Distinctly, the absorbance and fluorescence intensity both have a slight change, which prove that the addition of Arg to HAuCl4 solution before the formation of Au/CQDs composite is more sensitive for Arg detection. In addition, when compared to different sensing probes employed for Arg detection in recent years (Table 1), our dual-signal sensor has high sensitivity whether by fluorescence or UV–vis absorption method. Furthermore, both fluorescent and colorimetric methods show good linear relationship for Arg detection.

Then, the selectivity of this dual-signal sensor was surveyed by screening the spectral responses toward amino acid species, including Asn, Asp, Cys, Gln, His, Leu, Lys, Met, Phe, Pro, and Trp. As shown in Fig. 4, all the other amino acids do not interfere with the formation of Au/CQDs composite under same experimental conditions, and show little change in color, absorbance, and fluorescence. It could be attributed to the fact that all these amino acids do not have the guanidine group. The results prove that the present sensing system has a highly selective response to Arg, and the detection of Arg could be visualized by the color change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of arginine in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of this method for the detection of Arg, the concentrations of Arg in urine samples were examined. The samples were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min to remove particulates, while the addition of 20-fold of urine sample to the HAuCl4 solution does not lead to the obvious change in the formation of Au/CQDs composite. Then the urine samples at 20-fold dilution with known concentration of Arg were directly subjected to the HAuCl4 solutions. Subsequently, phosphate buffer and CQDs were added. The concentrations of Arg in urine samples can be calculated on the basis of the absorption decrease and fluorescence enhancement using the obtaining information. Fig. S10 shows the absorption and fluorescence responses of Au/CQDs composite in the presence of urine samples containing different concentrations of Arg. The recovery of the spiked samples ranged between 106.3% and 109.6% indicate that our strategy has the potential for determining Arg in biological samples (Table S1).

In summary, this paper reports a simple and stable Arg sensor which can be performed by the fluorescent and colorimetric methods. The proposed method can improve the sensitivity and selectivity for Arg detection. The new prepared CQDs acted as both reducing agent and stabilizer to synthesize Au/CQDs composite by virtue of abundant hydroxy and carboxy groups on the surface of CQDs. But by mixing Arg with the HAuCl4 solution before the addition of the CQDs, the formation of Au/CQDs composite was restrained due to the existence of guanidine group and electrostatic interaction. In addition, this study not only utilizes dual-signal for Arg detection but also provides an easy assay for rapid detection of Arg in real urine samples, which has a great potential for Arg detection in clinical and real analysis/monitoring in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174) and the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2013jjB00002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.08.098.
